name: Nim ARC / ORC — Hooks, Sink/Lent, YRC Threadsafe Cycle Collector
summary: Nim's `--gc:arc` desugars `ref` into hook-driven RC + move semantics; `--gc:orc` adds a Bacon-style trial-deletion cycle collector. Hooks (`=destroy`, `=copy`, `=sink`, `=trace`, `=deepcopy`) plus `sink`/`lent` parameters drive copy-elision and ownership transfer. The proposed YRC threadsafe cycle collector (PR #25495) is an active design effort.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Nim's `--gc:arc` desugars `ref` into hook-driven RC + move semantics; `--gc:orc` adds a Bacon-style cycle collector running on suspected cyclic types. `.cursor` and `.acyclic` annotations let the user statically prove an edge can't form a cycle, removing it from ORC's trial-deletion roots — a hand-written escape hatch.

ARC alone leaks cycles; ORC catches them via Bacon trial-deletion. ARC is competitive with manual C in seq-heavy code; ORC adds latency-stable cycle scans. The pair give Nim a unified RC-with-optional-cycle-collection model that is configurable per-build.

Status (as of 2026-04): ARC and ORC are production for several Nim 1.x and 2.x releases; YRC (PR #25495) is an active design effort, not established Nim behaviour.

sections.title: Hooks, sink and lent parameters
sections.content:
Type-bound hooks let the programmer customise RC traffic without touching the compiler:

- `=destroy(x)` — runs at end-of-life; default frees the memory and recurses into fields.
- `=copy(dst, src)` — runs for assignments that cannot be rewritten as moves; default does a deep `inc`-bumping copy.
- `=sink(dst, src)` — runs for assignments where `src` is a last use; default moves the bits and skips `inc`.
- `=trace(x, env)` — for ORC, walks reachable references during cycle scans.
- `=deepcopy(x)` — explicit user-requested deep copy.

Control-flow `lastReadOf` analysis converts copies to moves wherever possible, so the `=sink` hook fires far more often than `=copy` in idiomatic code. Parameter modes complete the picture: `sink T` parameters take ownership (the callee must `=destroy` or move-out), and `lent T` returns a hidden borrow that emits no destructor at the call site. The combination gives Nim a full move/borrow vocabulary on top of the RC substrate.

sections.title: ORC trial-deletion and the YRC threadsafe proposal
sections.content:
ORC adds a Bacon-style trial-deletion cycle collector. Suspected-cyclic types (those whose `=trace` hook is non-trivial and whose graph cannot be statically proved acyclic) accumulate roots when their RC drops without reaching zero; periodically the collector runs trial deletion: tentatively remove each root's contribution, walk the subgraph, and reclaim everything that becomes unreachable. `.cursor` and `.acyclic` annotations exclude edges from the suspect set, shrinking the work the collector has to do.

The proposed **YRC threadsafe cycle collector** (PR #25495) extends ORC to multi-threaded heaps. It explores striped per-thread queues for acyclic atomic RC plus a single global Bacon collector for cyclic refs, with deferred RC updates and TLA+ model-checked invariants. The TLA+ work is notable: Nim's mainline ARC/ORC is heavily tested but not formally modelled, and YRC's authors are using model checking to convince themselves the deferred-update protocol is race-free before merging.

Status (as of 2026-04): YRC is active design / implementation effort, not established Nim behaviour. If it lands, Nim will join Lean 4 and Roc in the small group of languages with a thread-safe compile-time-RC story; the alternative — atomic ops on every inc/dec everywhere — is what Swift pays.
